methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Growth → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $703M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Defense & Security (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Defense & Security, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
Improbable Defence, a Growth-stage defense and security company founded by Herman Narula, has raised $703M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 10 private and 2 public peers in the Defense & Security sector, including Anduril Industries, Scale AI, Shield AI, and others.

Stage-progression analysis, the sole applicable triangulation method, anchors valuation to the $703M total raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 1.2x step-up multiplier typical of Growth-to-next-stage transitions, yielding $844M. The peer-median approach proved inapplicable: while 21 Defense & Security peers exist broadly, the narrow filters for same-stage, adjacent-stage, and similar-tier companies yielded fewer than three datapoints required for reliable cross-sectional analysis. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce unacceptable noise.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $717M to $970M, with a median point estimate of $844M. This narrow methodological set—driven by stage and sector specificity constraints—introduces material uncertainty around capital efficiency multiples and comparable growth trajectories. Key sensitivities include the appropriateness of the 1.2x multiplier to Improbable's specific profile and the degree to which the limited peer set reflects true market dynamics for frontier-tech defense applications. Additional clarity on revenue, burn rate, and customer concentration would materially improve precision.
